summary: I met Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. the year I was living in the Southtown Housing Authority. He gave a powerful talk to all the black men about taking charge of their homes, which really made me rethink how I was managing my family and finances. His words inspired me to start making changes, like asking my children what they wanted for Christmas instead of my wife just buying what she thought was best. Dr. King’s leadership was a catalyst for many of the actions we took during the civil rights struggle in Birmingham.

We were living in the Southtown Housing Authority when I first met Dr. King. It was a tough place, a project where many black families lived, including mine with three boys and a girl. It was there that I began to understand the importance of taking control of my home and family finances after Dr. King's talk. Eventually, I started house hunting to move out of the projects into a better neighborhood.

Every Christmas, my wife would buy my boys Roy Rogers suits, and my daughter got a Dale Evans suit. We spent hundreds of dollars each year on these gifts, which was a lot for us living in the projects. I realized later that my wife was buying what she wanted for the kids, not what they actually wanted. That realization came after Dr. King's talk and changed how we approached Christmas gifts in our family.

My daughter used to get a Dale Evans suit every Christmas, just like my boys got Roy Rogers suits. This was part of the tradition my wife kept, spending a lot of money on what she thought the kids should have. But after Dr. King's talk, I started asking my children what they really wanted, which changed how we celebrated Christmas. It was a small but meaningful shift in our family life.

I decided to move to College Hill, which is tied in with Smithfield but near Center Street, the dividing line between black and white neighborhoods. No black families lived on the white side of Center Street because houses would get bombed if they did. But I wanted to live among white folks, so I bought a house there despite the risks. It was a difficult decision, but I wanted a home for my family outside the projects.

Smithfield is connected to College Hill, where I moved to escape the projects. It was part of the area where white folks lived, and moving there was a big step because black families were not welcomed on that side of Center Street. The neighborhood was mostly white, and tensions were high when we tried to integrate the community. It was a challenging but necessary move for my family.

Center Street was the dividing line between black and white neighborhoods in Birmingham. Any black family that moved past Center Street into white areas risked having their house bombed. Despite this, I chose to move near Center Street in College Hill to live in a better home. The hostility around that area was intense, but it was part of the struggle for integration and equality.

Reverend Fred Shuttlesworth had already tested school integration at Phillips High School, but he was beaten badly for it. That was a clear sign of the resistance we faced in Birmingham. It showed the risks involved in trying to integrate schools, but it also inspired us to volunteer and prepare for non-violent protest. Phillips High was a significant part of the civil rights struggle here.

Reverend Fred Shuttlesworth was a key figure in Birmingham's civil rights movement. He had already tested school integration at Phillips High School and suffered a severe beating for it. He organized mass meetings and called for volunteers to participate in non-violent protests, including integrating lunch counters and schools. His leadership was crucial in our fight for equality and justice.

The Brown v. Board of Education decision was the legal foundation for our efforts to integrate Birmingham schools. Reverend Shuttlesworth had already tested the '54 decision at Phillips High School, which was part of the broader struggle to enforce desegregation. Our family filed to integrate schools following that ruling, which led to a long legal battle and significant resistance from the community and authorities.

Graymont Elementary was the school my two younger boys attended when we integrated the schools in Birmingham. We filed the case starting around 1957, and after a six-year legal process, the court ordered the schools to integrate in 1963. My boys faced a lot of hostility there, including physical abuse, but they persevered and eventually were accepted by their peers. Graymont was at the center of our family's fight for educational equality.

Birmingham was the battleground for much of my family's civil rights struggle. From living in the Southtown Housing Authority to moving into white neighborhoods near Center Street, and fighting to integrate local schools, Birmingham was the backdrop of all these events. The city was deeply segregated and hostile, with figures like Governor George Wallace and Bull Connor opposing integration. Despite the challenges, Birmingham shaped our fight for justice and equality.

September 15, 1963, was a tragic day in Birmingham. It was five days after my boys started attending the integrated school, and tensions were very high. That day, the 16th Street Baptist Church was bombed, killing four young girls. The bombing was a devastating response to the progress we were making, and it marked a dark moment in our struggle for civil rights.

The 16th Street Baptist Church bombing happened just five days after my boys started attending the integrated school in Birmingham. It killed four girls and was a brutal act of hatred from those opposed to integration. The bombing shocked the community and showed the violent resistance we faced. It was a painful reminder of the cost of fighting for civil rights.

My oldest son attended Parker High School, which was part of the integrated school system in Birmingham. From Parker, he went to Boston on a French program and met a white family in Lincoln, Massachusetts, who later helped my boys with housing and education. Parker was the starting point for his journey beyond Birmingham and a symbol of the opportunities that integration could bring despite the challenges.

Boston became a second home for my boys after they left Birmingham. They lived with a white family in Lincoln, a suburb of Boston, who welcomed them and supported their education through high school and college. My wife and I visited Boston often and stayed with them in their home. The kindness and acceptance we found there contrasted sharply with the hostility we faced in Birmingham.

Lincoln is a suburb of Boston where my boys lived with a white family who took them in during their high school and college years. The head of the household was a principal of an elementary school there, and the family were Quakers who believed in equality and kindness. Living in Lincoln helped my boys grow and learn in a supportive environment far from the struggles in Birmingham.

My son Dwight finished his college education at Tufts University in Massachusetts. He lived with the family in Lincoln while attending college, which was a blessing for us. Tufts represented a new chapter for him, made possible because of the opportunities that arose after we stood up for integration in Birmingham.

My son Floyd graduated from Boston College after living with the family in Lincoln, Massachusetts. Like his brother, he benefited from the support and kindness of that family, which helped him succeed academically. Boston College was part of the journey that started with our fight for school integration back in Birmingham.

Eastern Air Lines was the airline that provided my boys with round-trip tickets to come home to Birmingham for Christmas while they were in college. This was a special gift from the family who took them in, showing their generosity and care. It allowed us to stay connected despite the distance and hardships we endured back home.

The family in Lincoln, Massachusetts, who took in my boys were Quakers. They practiced what they preached about equality and kindness, not seeing color but seeing people as human beings. Their faith and values inspired them to open their home and hearts to my children during a difficult time. Their support was a beautiful example of humanity during the civil rights era.

Governor George Wallace was a staunch opponent of school integration in Alabama. He famously said that before integration came to this state, blood would run in the streets. His stance made the fight for civil rights in Birmingham even more dangerous and difficult. Wallace's resistance was a major obstacle we had to face during the early 1960s.

Bull Connor was the Birmingham official who enforced segregation and violently opposed the civil rights movement. He was known for his harsh tactics against those trying to integrate schools and public spaces. His actions made the struggle for equality in Birmingham very dangerous for families like mine. Connor's role was a symbol of the entrenched racism we were fighting against.
